title: Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
subtitle: The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, _Acropora_, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and [coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching).
caption:
Share of Caribbean reefs with Acropora corals present or dominant
The share of coral reefs in the Caribbean where major reef-building group of corals, Acropora, was dominant or present. The declines show the loss of coral cover as a result of pressures including water pollution, disease outbreaks, and coral bleaching.

Between 1950 and 2007, the prevalence of Acropora corals across Caribbean reefs shows a sustained downward trajectory, measured as the percentage of reefs where these corals were either present or dominant. The chart tracks two distinct metrics over this 57-year period: the broader presence of the coral group and the narrower instances where it remained the dominant species. In 1950, the data begins with the highest recorded values, showing Acropora present on roughly 90% of reefs and dominant on nearly 80%. Over the subsequent decades, both lines move lower, though the rate of decline varies. The "Present" line drops to approximately 65% by the mid-1960s before recovering slightly to near 75% around 1975. Following this temporary rise, the trend turns consistently downward, falling below 60% by 1980 and dropping beneath the 30% mark by 1990. The "Dominant" line follows a similar but steeper path, falling from its 1950 high to roughly 50% by 1960 and hovering near 45% through the mid-1960s. After a brief rise to 60% in 1975, the dominance metric collapses, falling to roughly 20% by 1990 and reaching its lowest point near 5% in the early 2000s. By the final data point in 2007, the share of reefs where Acropora is present sits just under 20%, while the share where it is dominant remains below 10%. The visual gap between the two lines narrows significantly over time, showing that while the coral group persists in some locations, its role as the primary reef-builder has largely disappeared across the region.
